era_context: Philip II of Macedon continues his expansion while the Greek city-states are divided by internal conflicts (Third Sacred War and Social War). The Persian Achaemenid Empire remains internally unstable. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing military sophistication. This year is notable for the birth of Alexander the Great.

events.title: Birth of Alexander the Great
events.description: Alexander III (later known as Alexander the Great) is born in Pella, the capital of Macedon, to King Philip II and Queen Olympias. According to ancient sources, his birth coincides with three significant events: the destruction of the Temple of Artemis at Ephesus by arson, the victory of Philip II over the Illyrians, and the victory of the Macedonian general Parmenion over the Persians. Alexander's birth is of immense historical significance, as he will eventually conquer the Persian Empire and create one of history's largest empires. However, at the time of his birth, his future prominence is unknown.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's birth in 356 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The date is accepted by modern scholarship with high confidence, though some sources place it in 355 BCE with uncertainty of ±1 year.
cross_references: Leads to -336-001 (Philip's assassination and Alexander's accession)

events.title: Temple of Artemis at Ephesus destroyed by fire
events.description: The Temple of Artemis at Ephesus, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, is destroyed by fire. According to ancient sources, the temple is deliberately burned by Herostratus, who seeks immortal fame through the act. The destruction is a significant loss to the Greek world, as the temple is one of the largest and most magnificent structures of its time. The temple will be rebuilt, but the destruction marks a moment of cultural loss. The coincidence of this destruction with Alexander's birth is noted by ancient sources, though this is likely a later literary invention.

sources.name: Valerius Maximus, Memorable Deeds and Sayings

events.certainty_note: The destruction of the Temple of Artemis is attested in multiple ancient sources, but the precise date is debated. Some sources place it in 356 BCE, while others suggest different dates. The dating is uncertain by ±5–10 years.
cross_references: Leads to reconstruction of the temple

events.title: Philip II defeats the Illyrians at the Battle of Bardylis
events.description: Philip II achieves a major victory over the Illyrians, reportedly killing 7,000 enemy soldiers. This victory further secures Macedon's northern frontier and enhances Philip's military reputation. The victory is attributed to Philip's military innovations, particularly the development of the Macedonian phalanx. The battle is significant because it demonstrates the effectiveness of Philip's military reforms and establishes Macedon as a major military power in the Balkans.
events.certainty_note: The victory is attested in ancient sources, but the precise date is debated. Some sources place it in 356 or 355 BCE. The casualty figures (7,000) are likely exaggerated.
cross_references: Part of Philip's broader military expansion

events.title: Parmenion defeats the Persians in Asia Minor
events.description: Parmenion, a Macedonian general under Philip II, achieves a victory over Persian forces in Asia Minor near the Hellespont. This victory is significant because it represents the first Macedonian military success against the Persians and demonstrates that the Persian Empire is vulnerable to Macedonian military power. The victory is part of Philip's broader strategy to expand Macedonian influence and prepare for eventual conflict with Persia. Parmenion's success will make him one of Philip's most trusted generals and will lead to his prominent role in Alexander's later campaigns.
events.certainty_note: The victory is attested in ancient sources, but the precise date and location are uncertain. The event is assigned to 356 BCE by convention, but may have occurred in 355 or 357 BCE.
cross_references: Part of Philip's broader expansion strategy
Leads to Alexander's later Persian campaigns

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Alexander's birth is debated, with some sources placing it in 355 BCE rather than 356 BCE. The coincidence of Alexander's birth with the destruction of the Temple of Artemis and Philip's military victories is likely a later literary invention rather than historical fact. The casualty figures for Philip's battles are likely exaggerated. Parmenion's victory over the Persians is attested in sources, but the precise date and significance are debated; some scholars argue that the victory was minor and did not significantly threaten Persian control of Asia Minor.
historiographic_note: Sources for 356 BCE are primarily Greek and later Hellenistic compilations. The birth of Alexander is recorded in multiple sources, but these are written centuries after the event and may contain legendary elements. The destruction of the Temple of Artemis is attested in multiple sources, but the precise date is uncertain. Military events are recorded in later compilations that may not be entirely reliable. Persian sources are almost entirely absent, forcing reliance on Greek accounts. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Alexander's birth leads to his eventual accession to the throne
Alexander's life encompasses his conquests and empire
Part of Philip's broader military expansion
Parmenion's early victories against Persia foreshadow Alexander's later campaigns
